text: {"challenges":[{"challengeType":"scope-drift","targetKind":"claim","targetIndex":5,"topic":"","rationale":"The claim that AI offers \"significant benefits\" (personalized learning, improved outcomes, enhanced engagement) is based on data from a purposive sample of 85 students from two engineering programs, which the authors themselves note is not generalizable. Extrapolating to all educational contexts exceeds the scope of the evidence.","suggestedRemedy":"Qualify the claim to reflect the limited sample or provide additional data from broader, more diverse populations."},{"challengeType":"omitted-qualification","targetKind":"claim","targetIndex":6,"topic":"","rationale":"The listed challenges (over‑reliance, diminished critical thinking, privacy risks, academic dishonesty) are derived from self‑reported concerns, not from observed incidents. The source does not indicate that these concerns have manifested as actual harms, so the qualification that they are perceived rather than proven is omitted.","suggestedRemedy":"Add a qualifier such as \"perceived\" or \"self‑reported\" to indicate that the challenges are based on participant perceptions."},{"challengeType":"invalid-inference","targetKind":"inference","targetIndex":0,"topic":"","rationale":"Inference [0] treats participants' self‑reported improvements in self‑efficacy, engagement, and time‑saving as empirical evidence that AI provides \"significant advantages\". Perception does not equal objective performance improvement, making the inference logically unsupported.","suggestedRemedy":"Support the inference with objective performance metrics or reframe it as a perception‑based observation."},{"challengeType":"missing-source","targetKind":"claim","targetIndex":11,"topic":"","rationale":"The claim that AI‑powered platforms have been shown to enhance engagement and performance lacks any cited study or data; no source is provided to substantiate the statement.","suggestedRemedy":"Cite peer‑reviewed research that directly measures the impact of adaptive learning systems on engagement and performance, or remove the claim."},{"challengeType":"correlated-evidence","targetKind":"inference","targetIndex":8,"topic":"","rationale":"Inference [8] asserts that triangulation of quantitative and qualitative data enhances credibility, yet both data types originate from the same questionnaire administered to the same participants, sharing the same sampling bias and measurement errors, thus not providing independent corroboration.","suggestedRemedy":"Obtain qualitative data from a distinct method (e.g., interviews, focus groups) or acknowledge the limitation of using a single instrument for triangulation."}]}
